skill.name: git-pushing
skill.description: Stage, commit, and push git changes with conventional commit messages. Use when user wants to commit and push changes, mentions pushing to remote, or asks to save and push their work. Also activates when user says "push changes", "commit and push", "push this", "push to github", or similar git workflow requests.
skill.summary: Automates staging, committing, and pushing the current Git branch.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The external command findings are true positives because the skill runs a Bash script that stages, commits, and pushes Git changes. The network and weak cryptography findings on SKILL.md:3 are false positives from descriptive text, but the script still performs a real network push through git push.

medium_findings.title: Automated Git Push Can Publish Unreviewed Changes
medium_findings.description: The script stages all working tree changes and pushes the current branch to origin. This is expected for the skill, but it can publish secrets, generated files, or unfinished work if the user does not review changes first.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script directly runs git add, git commit, and git push. The behavior is legitimate for this skill, but the broad staging and remote push create clear publication risk.

medium_findings.title: Skill Instructions Require Shell Script Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill documentation tells the agent to run the Bash script for the workflow. This confirms the external command findings, although the commands are Git operations rather than unrelated system access.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation explicitly instructs shell execution. The context shows intended Git automation, so this is elevated operational risk rather than malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Network and Weak Crypto Text Matches
low_findings.description: SKILL.md:3 contains a natural-language description with activation phrases such as push requests and GitHub. I did not find evidence of Python HTTP libraries or weak cryptographic algorithms on that line.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line is frontmatter text, not executable code. The file context does not show crypto functions or Python HTTP imports.

dangerous_patterns.title: State-Changing Shell Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The Bash script changes repository state and contacts a remote by running Git commands. The branch variable is quoted, and no command injection evidence was found.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file is a shell script with explicit Git commands. The arguments are simple and quoted where variable data is used, reducing injection concern.

content.value_statement: Manual Git handoffs can miss staging, commit, or upstream push steps. This skill runs a defined workflow that stages, commits, and pushes the current branch.
actual_capabilities: Stages all current repository changes with git add.
Creates a Git commit with a provided or default message.
Detects the active branch before pushing.
Pushes the active branch to the origin remote with upstream tracking.
Documents a standard command for default and custom commit messages.
limitations: Does not inspect diffs or detect secrets before staging files.
Does not choose a remote other than origin.
Does not recover from failed commits, conflicts, or rejected pushes.
Does not verify that the commit message follows project policy.

use_cases.title: Push Completed Feature Work
use_cases.description: Stage, commit, and publish a finished feature branch after local review.

use_cases.title: Save Pair Programming Changes
use_cases.description: Commit shared coding session changes and push them to the remote branch.

use_cases.title: Standardize Small Maintenance Commits
use_cases.description: Use a repeatable commit and push flow for routine updates.

prompt_templates.title: Commit and Push Current Work
prompt_templates.prompt: Commit and push the current changes.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for the default workflow.

prompt_templates.title: Push With a Message
prompt_templates.prompt: Commit and push these changes with the message: chore: update project files.
prompt_templates.scenario: User provides a specific commit message.

prompt_templates.title: Publish Feature Branch
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the current branch name, then commit and push this feature branch.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a Conventional Commit
prompt_templates.prompt: Use a conventional commit message for these changes and push the branch to origin.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request for a standard team workflow.

output_examples.output: The skill stages the current changes, creates a commit, detects the branch, and pushes it to origin.

output_examples.input: Push this with the message feat: add export flow.
output_examples.output: The skill uses the provided message for the commit and pushes the active branch with upstream tracking.
The skill treats the request as a Git push workflow and publishes the current branch to the configured origin remote.

best_practices: Review git status and diffs before running the skill.
Check for secrets or generated files before staging all changes.
Use clear conventional commit messages for shared repositories.
anti_patterns: Do not run the skill with unreviewed local changes.
Do not use it for repositories without a configured origin remote.
Do not rely on it to resolve merge conflicts or rejected pushes.

faq.question: Does this skill push to GitHub?
faq.answer: It pushes to the configured origin remote, which may be GitHub or another Git host.

faq.question: Can I provide a custom commit message?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented script accepts a message argument and uses it for the commit.

faq.question: Does it review files before committing?
faq.answer: No. It stages all changes and does not inspect file contents.

faq.question: Does it support other remotes?
faq.answer: No. The script pushes to origin only.

faq.question: Is this safe for private repositories?
faq.answer: It can be used in private repositories, but users should review changes before pushing.

faq.question: What happens if the push fails?
faq.answer: The Bash script stops on errors and does not implement recovery steps.
